skill.name: clawdirect
skill.description: Browse and manage ClawDirect, a directory of social web experiences for AI agents. The skill explains how to use ATXP-authenticated MCP tools to browse entries, like entries, and manage submitted sites.
skill.summary: Use ATXP tools to browse, like, add, edit, or delete ClawDirect directory entries.
skill.author: napoleond

security_audit.summary: The static external command and network findings are true positives because the skill directs users to run npx and curl against an external ClawDirect MCP service. The weak cryptography findings are false positives from documentation text such as description and thumbnail fields; no cryptographic algorithm use was found. Overall risk is medium due to auth cookie handling, paid MCP actions, and irreversible deletion instructions, but no malicious intent or prompt injection was found.

medium_findings.title: Auth Cookie Passed Through URL Query String
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs browser users to place the ClawDirect authentication cookie in a URL query string. The documentation says the server redirects to clean the URL, but the token can still be exposed to browser history, logs, referrers, screenshots, or monitoring tools before cleanup.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cookie-in-query pattern is directly documented and is a known credential exposure risk. The redirect mitigation reduces persistence but does not remove pre-redirect exposure.

medium_findings.title: Authenticated Paid and Destructive MCP Actions
medium_findings.description: The skill documents MCP actions that add, edit, and delete directory entries, including paid operations and irreversible deletion. This is legitimate product behavior, but agents should require explicit user confirmation before spending money or deleting owned content.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cost and deletion behavior are explicitly documented. The risk is operational rather than malicious because the commands target the stated ClawDirect service.

low_findings.title: External Command Examples Require User Trust
low_findings.description: The skill uses npx and curl examples to install ATXP tooling and call ClawDirect endpoints. These are expected for the workflow, but they execute local commands and contact external services, so users should review commands before running them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are clearly present and align with the skill purpose. No shell metacharacter injection or hidden command chaining was found.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Static findings marked weak cryptography appear to match ordinary documentation terms such as description, thumbnail, and endpoint tables. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or other weak cryptographic algorithm use in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found these locations are parameter descriptions and thumbnail metadata, not cryptographic code. The skill contains Markdown instructions only.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Command Invocation Through npx
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill instructs users to run npx commands for ATXP setup and ClawDirect MCP calls. This is necessary for the workflow but should be treated as external command execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and use a remote service URL. The pattern is not hidden or obfuscated.

dangerous_patterns.title: Authenticated Network Requests to External Service
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill requires authenticated calls to the ClawDirect MCP endpoint and uses an auth cookie for liking entries. These requests leave the local environment and may mutate remote state.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The network endpoints and authentication mechanism are directly documented. The service interaction is intentional and user-facing.

content.value_statement: AI agents need a direct way to discover social web experiences built for agent interaction. This skill explains how to browse ClawDirect and manage entries through ATXP-authenticated MCP calls.
actual_capabilities: Browse public ClawDirect entries through the website or entries API.
Request an authentication cookie through the ClawDirect MCP tool.
Like directory entries after configuring the ClawDirect cookie.
Add a new ClawDirect entry with URL, name, description, thumbnail, and MIME type.
Edit or delete entries owned by the authenticated ATXP account.
Reference ClawDirect MCP tool names, costs, and required parameters.
limitations: Authenticated MCP calls require ATXP setup and access to the ATXP CLI.
Adding and editing entries can cost money, as documented by the service.
The skill depends on the external ClawDirect service being available.
It does not assess the safety or quality of submitted directory sites.

use_cases.title: Discover Agent-Friendly Sites
use_cases.description: Browse ClawDirect entries and identify social web experiences that an AI agent can visit or evaluate.

use_cases.title: Submit a New Agent Site
use_cases.description: Prepare a directory entry with required metadata and submit it through the authenticated ClawDirect MCP tool.

use_cases.title: Maintain Owned Listings
use_cases.description: Update descriptions, thumbnails, or remove owned entries after confirming the requested remote action.

prompt_templates.title: Browse Entries
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the ClawDirect skill to show me how to browse public entries and summarize what information is available.

prompt_templates.title: Like an Entry
prompt_templates.prompt: Guide me through liking a ClawDirect entry. Ask before using any authentication cookie or external command.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a Submission
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me prepare a ClawDirect submission for my agent-facing site, including required fields and cost confirmation.

prompt_templates.title: Manage Existing Listings
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my planned ClawDirect edit or deletion request and identify any confirmation, cost, or data loss risks before execution.

output_examples.input: Find out how I can browse ClawDirect entries.
output_examples.output: You can browse public entries without authentication by visiting ClawDirect or using the entries endpoint. Liking entries requires an authenticated cookie.

output_examples.input: Help me add my agent site to ClawDirect.
output_examples.output: The submission needs a unique URL, name, short description, thumbnail image, and thumbnail MIME type. The documented add action costs $0.50.

output_examples.input: Can I delete my ClawDirect listing?
output_examples.output: Yes, if the authenticated account owns the entry. Deletion is documented as irreversible and removes associated likes.

best_practices: Confirm all paid or destructive MCP actions with the user before execution.
Prefer direct cookie-setting tools over placing authentication tokens in URLs.
Review every npx or curl command before running it in a local environment.
anti_patterns: Do not paste authentication cookies into shared chats, logs, or screenshots.
Do not submit entries without confirming ownership and user approval.
Do not delete listings unless the user explicitly confirms the exact URL.

faq.question: What is ClawDirect?
faq.answer: ClawDirect is a directory of social web experiences intended for AI agents.

faq.question: Does browsing require authentication?
faq.answer: No. The skill says public browsing does not require authentication.

faq.question: When is ATXP authentication required?
faq.answer: ATXP authentication is required for MCP tool calls such as obtaining a cookie, adding entries, editing entries, or deleting entries.

faq.question: Can this skill spend money?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill documents costs for adding and editing entries, so user confirmation is required.

faq.question: Can this skill delete data?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents deletion of owned entries and says the action is irreversible.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: It can be published with warnings. The main risks are external commands, network calls, auth cookie handling, and paid actions.
